summary: This lesson introduces the 'go verbs' — irregular verbs that use -go instead of -o for the I form in the present tense (tengo, vengo, salgo, pongo, digo, hago, oigo, traigo). It explains that in the subjunctive, these verbs go from -go to -ga for all persons, and introduces traer (to bring) and its compound forms (-tract words).

teachings.summary: A group of irregular verbs use -go for the I form in the present tense, but only for I — all other persons are normal (or have their own stem changes). The verbs include: tener (tengo), venir (vengo), salir (salgo), poner (pongo), decir (digo), hacer (hago), oír (oigo), traer (traigo).
teachings.rule: Go verbs: -go only appears in the I form. He/she/they etc. conjugate normally (tiene, viene, sale, etc.).

teachings.conceptName: Go verbs in the subjunctive (-go to -ga)
teachings.summary: In the present subjunctive, go verbs change -go to -ga as the base for ALL persons (unlike the present where -go is only for I). So tengo → tenga, vengo → venga, traigo → traiga, etc.
teachings.rule: -go → -ga for subjunctive base. Tenga (I/he/she), tengas (you informal), tengamos (we), tengan (they).

examples.english: I want you to bring it
I don't want you to distract me
Tell me (formal) — used when answering the phone in Spain

teachings.notes: Venga is used colloquially in Spain as 'come on'. Dígame is used to answer the phone in Spain.

teachings.summary: Traer means 'to bring', but in compound verbs it translates as -tract: extraer (extract), atraer (attract), contraer (contract), distraer (distract). All follow the same -go pattern (distraigo, etc.).
teachings.rule: Traer in compounds = -tract in English. All compounds follow the traigo/-traiga pattern.

vocabulary.derivation: I form: traigo. Related to -tract compounds
